questions.question: Which body is the UK regulator responsible for data protection?
options: The Information Commissioner's Office (ICO)
Skills for Care
The Care Quality Commission (CQC)
The local coroner

questions.question: When someone asks to see a copy of the records held about them, within what period must the provider respond?
options: One week
One month
One year
Three months

questions.question: For how long are adult social care records generally kept, as a minimum, following an organisation's retention policy?
options: Eight years
Five years
Three years
Twenty years

questions.question: How can a subject access request be made?
options: Only by a solicitor on the person's behalf
Either verbally or in writing
Only in person at the office
Only in writing on an official form

questions.question: What are the Caldicott principles best described as?
options: A method for writing daily notes in black ink
A guide for handling confidential health and care information and judging when sharing is appropriate
A checklist for how long to keep paper records
A set of rules for disposing of electronic devices

questions.question: What does confidentiality mean when handling care records?
options: Personal information can be shared with any colleague who is curious
Personal information is kept secret even from the person it is about
Personal information is shared only with those who have a genuine need to know it for the person's care
Personal information is never shared with anyone

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a personal data breach?
options: Recording only relevant, necessary information
Passing a change in a person's condition to their GP for their care
A care record emailed to the wrong address
Logging out of a device before leaving it

questions.question: Because they are so sensitive, health and care records count as which type of data under the UK GDPR?
options: Ordinary personal data
Anonymous data
Special category data
Public data

questions.question: What should you write paper records in?
options: Blue erasable ink
Permanent black ink
Any colour, as long as it is neat
Pencil, so mistakes can be rubbed out

questions.question: You need to correct a mistake in an electronic record. What is the correct approach?
options: Delete the wrong entry so it no longer appears
Add a correcting entry and let the system's timestamp and your login show who amended it and when
Leave the mistake because electronic records cannot be edited
Ask a colleague to log in and change it for you

questions.question: What does the data protection principle of 'data minimisation' mean in everyday recording?
options: Use the fewest possible documents in the setting
Write records as quickly as possible using abbreviations
Keep records for the shortest possible time
Record what is relevant and necessary, not everything you know

questions.question: Why should you never let a colleague record an entry using your login?
options: It uses up the account's storage space
Entries are linked to you, so anything recorded under your login is treated as done by you
It slows the electronic system down
Colleagues are not allowed to write records at all

questions.question: You finish a visit and realise the printed daily notes are missing and may have been left at a previous person's home. What should you do first?
options: Tell your manager straight away and explain exactly what happened
Ask the previous person to post them back before mentioning it
Wait a few days to see if the notes turn up
Rewrite the notes from memory and say nothing

questions.question: Why should you avoid leaving blank lines or gaps in a paper record?
options: It wastes paper and ink
The pages will not scan into the electronic system
It makes the page look untidy for inspectors
Someone could write in them later, altering the record

questions.question: Confidentiality means that personal information is never shared with anyone else under any circumstances.
options: True
False

questions.question: Electronic care records keep an automatic audit trail showing who made or amended an entry and when.
options: True
False

questions.question: A subject access request must always be made in writing.
options: True
False

questions.question: Serious personal data breaches may need to be reported to the ICO, sometimes within a tight time limit.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following are Caldicott principles for handling confidential information? (Select all that apply)
options: Justify the purpose for sharing information
Keep all records for at least twenty years
Share on a need-to-know basis with those involved in the person's care
Use only the minimum information necessary
Record everything you know about a person, just in case

questions.question: Which of the following are examples of a personal data breach? (Select all that apply)
options: A paper care file left on a bus
A care record emailed to the wrong address
Sharing a change in condition with the district nurse to deliver care
A private conversation about a person overheard in a corridor
Logging out of a tablet when stepping away from it

questions.question: Which of the following are good habits for keeping devices and paper records secure? (Select all that apply)
options: Lock or log out of a device whenever you step away from it
Leave records open on an unattended screen
Keep your login password private and never write it on a sticky note
Return paper notes to secure storage and keep them out of sight
Share your login so colleagues can help enter notes

questions.question: Which of the following are 'don'ts' of good record keeping? (Select all that apply)
options: Record refusals and concerns, not just what went well
Sign, date and time each entry
Guess or exaggerate what happened
Write entries in advance before care has happened
Use correction fluid to hide a mistake

questions.question: The UK regulator for data protection is the Information Commissioner's Office, usually known by its initials, the ___.
Adult social care records are generally kept for a minimum of ___ years, in line with the organisation's retention policy.
When a person asks for a copy of the personal information held about them, this is called a ___ access request.
